description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird), and explain the reasons those symbols are used

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state in the Northeast became a state, and explain why

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and capitals in the Northeastern region of the United States (Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Vermont, and the District of Columbia) and where they are located on a map

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states that make up the Northeastern region including their admittance to the United States and their identities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the Northeast (e.g., granite, iron ore, lumber, fish - freshwater and marine) and explain their importance to the region

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns in areas of the Northeast

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of the Northeast (e.g., Atlantic coastline, Appalachian Mountains, woodlands, Hudson River, eastern portion of the Great Lakes) on a map and explain their importance to the region

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of the Northeastern region of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major imports and exports of the United States, and explain which countries are trading partners

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries of the United States (e.g., healthcare, education services, finance and insurance, manufacturing, technology), and analyze the ways they support jobs and the economy

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the United States, and analyze what the data describes about the country

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the population and industries in the United States today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the United States (e.g., Flag of the United States, National anthem, bald eagle, Great Seal of the United States, words of the Pledge of Allegiance, National motto), and explain the reasons those symbols are used and their meanings

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the differences between rules and laws

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the type of government the United States has and analyze the reasons we have a government

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify who state representatives are (e.g., governors, senators) and explain that they are elected positions, and that they represent the people of their states

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of the President, Vice President, and other Cabinet leaders (e.g., Secretary of State, Secretary of the Treasury, Secretary of the Interior), and identify the current people who hold those positions

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of the capital of the United States and the White House

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze components of the United States government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways states and territories differ and the rights afforded to those born in each area (e.g., political rights, voting rights)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the regions that make up the United States on a map including their states and territories

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what states and territories are and their locations within the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify land bordering countries to the United States (e.g., Canada, Mexico), and explain relationships the U.S. has with them (e.g., trade, ways leaders work together, sharing resources)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of North America (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain features of a map (e.g., cardinal directions, key/legends, map scales, latitude and longitude)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography of the United States and its neighbors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the work of these social scientists establishes knowledge of human development throughout time and peoples' relationships with each other and the environment

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that oral traditions inform what we know about the past

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways anthropologists work with people today to learn about their cultures

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain approaches historians take to analyze and interpret the past using primary and secondary sources

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that archeologists study the physical evidence left behind by humans to understand human culture

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how geographers use tools to understand the features of the globe and the inhabitants of the Earth

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways social scientists piece together information to have knowledge of history and the world today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify issues that Rhode Islanders are debating today at the local and state levels (e.g., affordable housing, funding for education, taxes, access to healthcare), and analyze the reasons why these issues are important to Rhode Island communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that students can participate in their local and state governments despite not being of voting age (e.g., writing letters to state or local leaders, speaking at local council meetings)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Rhode Island's voting process and who is allowed to vote

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways Rhode Islanders can participate in local and state governments today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways children in Rhode Island have participated in some of these movements (e.g., student walkouts at Hope and Central High Schools for Black student rights in the 1960s, young girls participating in woman suffrage demonstrations in the 1910s, Hope High School students forming the Providence Student Union in the 2010s), and analyze the role children can play in government today

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways different groups of people gained civil rights in Rhode Island and how they gained those rights (e.g., Narragansett peoples regaining tribal land, Gay Pride Parade for LGTBQIA+ recognition and rights, textile union strikes, Black Rhode Islanders working on fair housing laws)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the history of voting rights in Rhode Island and how people participated to gain those rights (e.g., woman suffrage, Dorr Rebellion)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways Rhode Islanders participated in state government in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how local laws are made and enforced, and ways people can participate in lawmaking at the local level (e.g., vote, send an email to the mayor, speak at a town/city council meeting)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location(s) of students' town/city hall(s) and explain what decisions are made there

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify city/town leaders and explain how they are elected and what issues they address

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain local city or town government, rules, and laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how laws are made at the state level, why we have laws, and ways people can participate in lawmaking (e.g., sign a petition, write a letter to a representative, testify at a hearing, vote)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of the Rhode Island State House and explain what decisions are made there

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the Rhode Island representatives in the United States Senate and House of Representatives and explain what issues they address

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the state-level leaders who represent students' district(s) and explain how they are elected and what issues they address

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Rhode Island's state government, rules, and laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify landmarks and monuments that honor historical events and people in Rhode Island and explain their importance

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that diverse backgrounds influence the culture of Rhode Island today (e.g., Indigenous Johnny cakes, Italian calamari, Portuguese bread)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that Rhode Islanders celebrate and continue cultural traditions (e.g., Narragansett August Meeting, Columbus Day Parade on Federal Hill, 4th of July Parade in Bristol)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the population and demographics of Rhode Island (e.g., gender, age, ethnic background, race) and explain why there are areas where there are concentrations of people

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of Rhode Island's cities and explain the reasons for their locations

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the industries and jobs in Rhode Island today (e.g., tourism, healthcare, education, military) and argue how they impact the state

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how industries, diverse peoples, and landmarks impact the development of state identity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conditions that led immigrant groups to come to Rhode Island to participate in those major industries and their contributions to the culture of Rhode Island (e.g., Cape Verdeans, Chinese, Dominicans, Guatemalans, Hmong, Italians, Irish, Portuguese, Puerto Ricans)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how major industries contributed to Rhode Island's economy into the late 20th century (e.g., fishing, agriculture, costume jewelry, textiles, banking, tourism)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major industries that contributed to Rhode Island's economy in the 19th and 20th centuries and how these industries encouraged people looking for opportunity to come to the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of immigration to Rhode Island during this era (e.g., Irish, French Canadians) and analyze the reasons people came to Rhode Island

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the textile industry's connection to southern slavery despite the end to slavery in the North

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rationale behind child labor, analyze labor conditions and efforts to end child labor (including Lewis Hine's visits to Rhode Island mills to document child labor), and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify reasons for changes in rural agricultural production to more factories and argue how this impacted families and communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways geography and the environment supported the development of a mill system, how Rhode Island's geography influenced the mill system and argue how this type of industry impacted the environment

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Samuel Slater's role in starting the Industrial Revolution in America and the structure of the "Rhode Island System of Manufacture"

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the development of the textile industry impacted Rhode Island economically, socially, and environmentally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons the addition of the Bill of Rights led Rhode Island to ratify the Constitution of the United States and become a state

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons Rhode Islanders did not go to the Constitutional Convention (rights of a smaller state, fear of a central authority)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the circumstances that lead to Rhode Island's eventual ratification of the Constitution of the United States and why it was the last of the thirteen colonies to do so.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the formation of and recruitment for the 1st Rhode Island Regiment (e.g., enslaved Black Rhode Islanders, free Black and Indigenous people), and analyze the reasons men decided to join the Regiment (e.g., promise of freedom after the war for enslaved soldiers, belief in the ideals of the Revolution)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the elements of the Gradual Emancipation Act, and analyze ways the abolition movement was tied to the ideals of the Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles Rhode Island women fulfilled during the American Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location and circumstances of the Battle of Rhode Island, and explain what happened

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions of the occupation of Newport, and analyze how it affected Newport's economy

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what privateers were, and analyze their role in establishing the Navy

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Rhode Islanders' participation in the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why Rhode Islanders joined the American Revolution, and analyze the ideals of the Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause of The Act of Renunciation of May 4, 1776, and argue how it was significant to the lead up to the American Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conditions leading to the Gaspee Affair, and argue how it was significant to the lead up to the American Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rationale for and conditions of the Sugar and Stamp Acts, and analyze how Rhode Islanders responded

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the acts and events leading to Rhode Island's participation in the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify free and enslaved Black Rhode Islanders from this time period and analyze their contributions to society (e.g., Duchess Quamino, Newport Gardner)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways enslaved and free Africans maintained culture and re-created a new culture (e.g., naming traditions, foods, music, religion, forming of social organizations such as the African Free Union Society, Negro elections)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways some Black Rhode Islanders obtained their freedom (e.g., buy their or family members' freedom, enslavers granting freedom)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the work enslaved people in Rhode Island were forced to do and what parts of Rhode Island they supported (e.g., farm labor on South County plantations, domestic labor in city households, labor on merchant shops)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways enslaved Africans resisted enslavement (e.g., insurrection on ships, refusal to work, self-emancipation)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the lives of free and enslaved Africans in Rhode Island.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Rhode Island's involvement in the trade of enslaved Africans, and argue who was complicit and who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conditions of the enslavement and trade of Africans, explain how the triangular trade functioned, and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods (e.g., furniture, rice, cotton, tobacco, spermaceti oil) traded with other American colonies (e.g., Massachusetts, Connecticut, colonies in the American South, English colonies in the Caribbean), explain their significance in the development of Rhode Island's economy, and argue the impacts of this trade on others

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods (e.g., chocolate, rum, spermaceti candles, cod, iron) exported to other parts of the world from Rhode Island, explain their significance in the development of Rhode Island's economy, and argue the impacts of this trade on others

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods imported into Rhode Island through maritime trade (e.g., mahogany, cacao beans, molasses, silk, porcelain, tea) and where they came from (e.g., Europe, Africa, Caribbean, India, China), explain their significance in the development of Rhode island's economy, and argue the impacts of this trade on others

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between Rhode Island's geographic position and the development of cities and maritime trade, and identify the location of major port cities on a map (e.g., Providence, Newport, Bristol)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of Rhode Island's relationship with the world through maritime trade, including participation in the trans-Atlantic slave trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions for expansion of English settlement and the growth and establishment of the Rhode Island colony and argue its impact on colonists and the Indigenous peoples

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain English-style forestry and agriculture and argue the ways those methods impacted the land

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the natural features of the area and argue how those features are related to the growth of the economy (e.g., the ocean for shipping, travel, and fishing; waterways for travel, trade, and fishing; forests for wood; soapstone quarries for bowls)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the uses of resources imported from England into the colony (e.g., livestock, furniture, grains, metal pots, weapons) and analyze the ways resources contributed to their lifeways

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples and analyze the ways the areas' natural resources were used to support settlement and a way of life (e.g., wood for building homes, available resources for food, clothing, and trade)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment supported a new way of life for Rhode Island Colonists while their lifeways in turn impacted the environment and Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze trade and land use "agreements" between Indigenous and English leaders (especially Roger Williams and the Narragansett) and argue who benefited from those agreements

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the system of indenture and "binding out" Indigenous children for indentured service and argue who benefited from that system

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the range of perspectives on land use of the Indigenous peoples and the English colonists

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of colonial encroachment on Indigenous lands, culture, and activities (e.g., instances of English settlers allowing their pigs to stomp on and eat Indigenous crops, fencing off hunting areas)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze major conflicts between the English colonists and the Indigenous people, identify who was involved, and argue who benefited from the outcomes (e.g., Pequot War, King Philip's War, Great Swamp Massacre, the sale of Indigenous people after the wars into slavery)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts early European explorers and tradesmen had on Indigenous populations (e.g., trade, diseases)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the relationships and conflicts between the Indigenous peoples and the English colonists in Rhode Island.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the range of other religious groups who settled in the Rhode Island colony (e.g., Quakers, Jews) and explain why they were welcomed to do so

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the rationale for and components of the charter John Clark and Roger Williams obtained from England (e.g., freedom of religion, elected government) and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the founders of other English settlements in the Rhode Island colony (e.g., Anne Hutchinson, William Coddington, Samuel Gorton, Richard Smith, and William Harris) and analyze their reasons for creating other settlements and the conditions of those settlements

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences between the town of Providence and other New England towns (e.g., religious beliefs, how home lots were divided) and argue the ways Williams' beliefs influenced the spatial layout of the town of Providence

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the components and rationale for Williams' "Civil Compact" and his ideas about religious freedom

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how Williams' religious beliefs informed the establishment of Providence, the addition of more settlements, and the formation of its early government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify other people who joined Roger Williams to settle Providence (e.g., Chad Brown, Alice Daniels, Richard Waterman, Thomas Olney) and explain their significance

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what the area of Providence was like and analyze the ways nearby resources supported the new settlement

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Williams' journey after banishment, who helped him, and analyze why he eventually settled in the location he named "Providence"

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Williams' beliefs about religion and analyze how those beliefs went against what leaders in Massachusetts Bay and Plymouth Bay colonies wanted colonists to believe and how they wanted them to worship

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Roger Williams' leadership, banishment from the Massachusetts colonies, and relocation to what is now known as Rhode Island.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze different ways we know and understand the past (e.g., oral traditions from Indigenous descendants, documentation from early explorers and colonists - including Roger Williams' A Key into the Language of America, historical records, archaeology, anthropology), and identify the biases of different types of sources

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze contemporary tribal governments and organizations of Indigenous people in Rhode Island today and ways the peoples today continue to practice cultural traditions

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships among Indigenous groups, including those who lived nearby (e.g., Pequot, Mohegan)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways Indigenous groups used available resources (e.g., fish for food; deer for food, clothing, tools; trees for homes, canoes; stones for tools) and analyze the relationship between geography and resource availability

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain cultural aspects of these Indigenous groups (e.g., language, art, clothing, homes, food, kinship system)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations of the Nahaganset (Narragansett), Nehantick and Eastern Nehantick (Niantic), Nipmuc, Manissean, Massachuset, and Wampanoag (Pokanoket) peoples on a map and analyze the relationship between geography and settlement patterns

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the history and culture of the Indigenous peoples who live in what is now known as Rhode Island.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states that border Rhode Island

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the natural resources available in Rhode Island (e.g., freshwater and marine fish, lumber, agriculture) and explain their significance

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major geographical features and environment of Rhode Island (e.g., climate, bodies of water) and explain their significance

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of Rhode Island including natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cities and towns that border those of the students

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations and explain the importance of parks in students' cities/towns

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the natural resources available in students' cities/towns

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the geographical features of students' cities/towns and explain the significance of each

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and features of students' cities / towns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the backlash to Reconstruction and the violence aimed at Black officeholding, economic independence, and culture creation (e.g., white supremacy's role in the rise of the KKK, Black Codes, 1872 Amnesty Act, the election of 1876)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the efficacy of the northern response to Reconstruction (e.g., Petition of Horace Greeley Wade 1866, Petition for equal rights 1870, Rhode Island General Law Chapter 508 in 1885, Reverend Mahlon Van Horne)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the social and economic changes as a result of ending enslavement of African Americans (e.g., rebuilding of Black families and culture, reuniting families through advertisements, Southern Homestead Act, sharecropping, "40 acres and a mule")

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of the Freedmen's Bureau and the Rhode Island Association for Freedmen in supporting formerly enslaved persons during Reconstruction (e.g., redistributing lands, establishing school systems, reuniting families, providing food and medical care), and argue how these organizations supported the work of bettering human rights

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the purpose for the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments, and argue their short- and long-term impacts

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the political advancement of the Black community (e.g., Congressional members during Reconstruction, Black members of state legislatures, voting registration and practices) and what obstructions were placed in their path

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the components and impact of Reconstruction

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the period immediately following the Civil War and the ways Black individuals and groups acted to resist white supremacy and advocate for their freedoms.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of how the Confederacy is remembered today

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the Emancipation Proclamation, General Order No.3, and Juneteenth

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the efficacy of Rhode Island's response to the war, (e.g., Ambrose E. Burnside, 14th Rhode Island Heavy Artillery (Colored), Katharine Prescott Wormeley, Julia Ward Howe, Rhode Island's factory production, Portsmouth Grove Military Hospital)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of various individuals and groups in the Civil War (e.g., free Blacks and enslaved Africans, women, children, LGBTQIA+ individuals, Indigenous peoples)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the advantages of the North (e.g., more men of military age, larger industrial capacity, strong centralized government, technological developments)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the battles and their effects on the course of the war (e.g., Antietam, Vicksburg, Gettysburg)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify leaders during the war (e.g., Abraham Lincoln, Ambrose Burnside, Ulysses S. Grant, Robert E. Lee, Stonewall Jackson), analyze their actions and contributions, and argue their impacts

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the events and participants of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the formation of the Confederate States of America, the states involved, and their reasons for secession

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways that slavery as a political, economic, and social institution divided the country

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the 1850s political crisis as the catalyst for secession (e.g., Compromise of 1850, Fugitive Slave Law 1850, Kansas-Nebraska Act 1854, Dred Scott decision 1857, Lincoln-Douglas debates, John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry 1859)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze politics pre-1850s and how it added to the tensions between the North and the South (e.g., Constitutional compromises, Missouri Compromise 1820, Wilmot Proviso, Free Soil Party 1848-1854)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the political precursors to the Southern secession from the Union and the start of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the continued racism against Black Americans in the North despite the end of slavery (e.g., limited access to education, turned away from employment, lack of suffrage, racial violence including the destruction of the neighborhoods of Hardscrabble and Snowtown in Rhode Island, personal stories like Elleanor Eldridge in Rhode Island)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze legal bids for freedom (e.g., Biddy Mason 1856, Dred Scott 1857, Archy Lee 1858), and argue their impact

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the implications of the Fugitive Slave Law of 1850

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the goals and strategies of the abolition movement (e.g., Olaudah Equiano, Frederick Douglass, Sojourner Truth, William Lloyd Garrison, Lydia Maria Child) and argue their impact

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the efficacy of resistance and revolts, (e.g., Stono Rebellion 1739, Nat Turner's Rebellion 1831) and stories of escape (e.g., Harriet Jacobs, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Tubman, Underground Railroad, including role of Elizabeth Buffum Chace in the Underground Railroad in Rhode Island)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways enslaved people resisted enslavement (e.g., self-emancipation, slowing down work, destruction of property) and maintained and created culture (e.g., music, clothing, religious beliefs)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the conditions of enslavement for enslaved Africans, (e.g., types of labor, living accommodations, Slave Codes), and analyze differences across the states

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conditions of enslavement and the many efforts of free and enslaved Blacks to obtain freedom for enslaved Africans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze other industries, and argue their influence on the United States (e.g., whaling, shipping, international trade)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events of the Industrial Revolution, and argue how the shift from an agricultural to industrial economy shaped daily life prior to the Civil War (e.g., Slater Mill in Rhode Island, Rhode Island System, Lowell Mill Girls, child labor, technological developments)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the definition of the Transportation Revolution, and argue its impact on the United States (e.g., steamboats, canals, roads, bridges, turnpikes, railroads)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how changes to transportation and industry in the first half of the 19th century impacted people's lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of the removal of Indigenous peoples from their lands by the United States government (e.g., Andrew Jackson's Indian Removal Acts (1830), Trail of Tears (1838), Indian Appropriations Act 1851, Dawes Act 1887, Indian Boarding Schools), and argue the impacts on community and family and who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the groups that immigrated and migrated to the East Coast, the Midwest, and the West Coast, analyze effects on immigrant community and family, and argue the impact on Indigenous peoples

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the laws governing immigration and citizenship (e.g., 14th Amendment 1868, Naturalization Act of 1870, Chinese Exclusion Act 1882, Rhode Island Bourn Amendment 1888, Immigration Act 1891) and argue how the laws impacted people

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the ways that migration, laws governing migration, and government actions forcing migration affected different groups of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the rationale and rules for establishing free and slave states, and argue the impacts on enslaved Africans

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and course of the Mexican American War 1846-1848 (e.g., Mexican freedom from Spain in 1821, Treaty of Guadalupe-Hidalgo 1848, California Constitution 1849), and Indigenous response and resistance, and argue the impacts on Mexican citizens and Indigenous peoples

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the route, purpose, and events of the Lewis and Clark expedition 1804-1806

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the efforts to gain land and expand territory (e.g., the Louisiana Purchase 1803, Spanish Florida 1810-1819, Texas Annexation 1845, Oregon Country from Great Britain 1846, Gadsden Purchase 1853), Indigenous response and resistance, and argue the ways Indigenous peoples were affected

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the definition of manifest destiny and analyze the justification for westward expansion

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the acquisition of land from the Atlantic to the Pacific had consequences for different peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the changing views of slavery in legislation (e.g., role of the Northwest Ordinance is limiting the spread of slavery, emancipation acts in Northern States including Rhode Island's Gradual Emancipation Act of 1784, "Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves" 1807), and argue the impacts of those views

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the limits of political participation (e.g., voting requirements, holding office rules, Alien and Sedition Acts 1798), and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the development of the two-party system, and analyze the resulting debates (e.g., Alexander Hamilton v. Thomas Jefferson, Federalists, Democratic-Republicans)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences among the first three presidents and their administrations (i.e., George Washington, John Adams, Thomas Jefferson)

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the political changes and uncertainties immediately following the ratification of the Constitution of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the elements of the Constitution (e.g., branches of government, checks and balances), and explain the purpose of the Bill of Rights

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of the institution of slavery in the drafting of the Constitution (e.g., 3/5th rule of representation, fugitive-slave clause, slave importation clause)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Articles of Confederation in terms of advantages and disadvantages

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the elements of the Constitution of the United States and its significance in forming a new United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the contributions of enslaved and free Blacks during this era (e.g., Rhode Island Black Regiment, Olaudah Equiano, Phillis Wheatley, Duchess Quamino, Newport Gardner)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze women's role as patriots (e.g., Deborah Sampson, Phillis Wheatley, homespun, spinning bees, camp followers)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the events of the war (e.g., Battle of Lexington and Concord 1775, Non-importation Agreements, Siege of Newport 1778, Battle of Rhode Island 1778, Battle of Yorktown 1781), and analyze their significance to the outcome

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons individuals and groups remained neutral (e.g., Quakers, Indigenous tribes such as the Six Nations)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and organizations who remained loyal to the Crown (e.g., Newport Gazette, Joseph Brant or Thayendanegea), and analyze the reasons for their loyalty

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the diversity of patriots (e.g., white men and women from all classes, free Blacks, enslaved peoples, and Indigenous peoples), and analyze their reasons for and ways of rebelling against the Crown

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the actions and activities of the participants of the Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that students see the ideals of the Declaration of Independence represented in their lives today

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influences of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy on the drafting of the Declaration of Independence (e.g., Great League of Peace)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the backgrounds (race, gender, occupation, religion, age, location, and view of slavery) of the Declaration of Independence signatories, and explain their perspectives and political views (e.g., Rhode Island's Stephen Hopkins and William Ellery)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the grievances and ideals laid out in the Declaration of Independence

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how liberty and natural rights are defined in the Declaration of Independence

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ideals of the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze protests and major events leading up to the American Revolution (e.g., Boston Massacre, Gaspee Affair, Stamp Act riots, North Carolina Regulators, various Tea Parties), and argue how these led to the start of Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Proclamation of 1763 and other acts of the British government meant to recoup financial costs of the French and Indian War (e.g., Sugar Act 1764, Currency Act 1764, Stamp Act 1765, Townshend Act 1767) and assert control over the colonies (e.g., Quartering Act 1765, Coercive Acts 1774) and argue how these led to the start of the Revolution

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the roles and contributions Indigenous peoples had in the French and Indian War

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the causes and course of the French and Indian War, and argue how it impacted the colonies

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how Britain's response at the end of the French and Indian War led to the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how race was socially constructed during this time, and argue the ways it can be traced to how race is socially constructed today

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the significant roles free Blacks had in the colonies (e.g., Anthony Johnson, John Quamino, Bristol Yamma), and explain their contributions

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions of slavery in colonial Rhode Island (e.g., first enslaved African in Rhode Island 1696 from ship Seaflower, role of Rhode Islanders in the slave trade, rural v. urban slavery, 1652 law limiting terms of servitude)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze laws and codes controlling enslaved Africans, free Blacks, indentured whites, and Indigenous peoples in the 1600-1700s and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role the system of slavery had in the economic development of the colonies, and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins and course of African enslavement in the colonies and the development of chattel slavery, and argue who benefited from this new form of slavery

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rationale local colonists used to enslave Indigenous peoples (e.g., enslavement of Pequot, Narragansett, and Wampanoag after the Pequot War 1637 and King Philip's War 1676, connection to African enslavement), and explain the conditions of enslavement

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts the new system of enslavement had on the economy and the people involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conflicts between Indigenous peoples and European colonists and their outcomes (e.g., Aztec Conquest 1520s, Pueblo Revolt 1680, Pequot War 1630s, King Philip's War 1670s, California Missions 1760s-1830s)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences between political, cultural, and trade relationships between French, Spanish, and English colonists with Indigenous communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the differences between Indigenous and European colonists' views on land ownership, land use, and property rights

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships between Indigenous peoples and European colonists and the evolving nature of those relationships.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the composition and ideologies of each colony including population and reason for immigration, governmental structures, economic systems, sources of labor, lifeways, religion, and relationships with Indigenous communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations and geography of the colonies across North America (New England, Middle Colonies, Southern Colonies, New France, New Spain), and analyze the reasons colonists decided to settle in those locations

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the colonies throughout North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the economic motivations of settler colonialism, and argue who benefited

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of the Columbian Exchange, and argue who benefited (e.g., introduction of invasive plant species to North America, introduction of animals such as pigs and horses, impact of diseases such as smallpox on Indigenous populations)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons colonial powers (e.g., Dutch, English, French, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Vikings) sailed to North America, their understanding of land ownership, and the initial responses of the Indigenous peoples they encountered, and argue the impacts of colonization

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the ways the motivations and methods of European colonization in North America affected the Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that Indigenous peoples continue their government and traditional practices today

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cooperation and conflict between Indigenous nations prior to European arrival

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cultures, government structures, and lived experiences (e.g., trade networks, kinship system, spiritual practices) of groups of Indigenous peoples across North America prior to the arrival of Europeans (e.g., Haudenosaunee, Huron, Cherokee, Navajo, Creek, Apache, Paiute, Pueblo, Hopi, Lakota (Sioux), Seminole, Taino, Inuit, Maya), including those local to Rhode Island (e.g., Nahaganset (Narragansett), Wampanoag (Pokanoket), Nehantick and Eastern Nehantick (Niantic), Pequot, Nipmuc, Massachuset, Mohegan, Manissean)

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geographical spread of Indigenous communities and language families, and analyze the relationship between geography and location of settlements (e.g., access to resources, climate)

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the lived experiences of Indigenous peoples prior to European colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations people settled, and analyze the reasons people chose those areas over others (e.g., access to resources, fertile land)

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the countries that make up North America today and their locations on a map

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major geographical features of North America (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts), and analyze their relationships with human settlement

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the countries, geographic features, and climates of North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities

subject: Social Studies

description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.

description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.

description: Analyze the status of population and power centers around the world in 1300 CE.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.

description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.

description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.

description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.

description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.

description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.

description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.

description: Identify methods for evaluating the credibility of online and print sources

description: Argue the benefits and challenges of digital news and social media

description: Explain the role of the press, and analyze how that role has changed over time, and the responsibilities it has to the public

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the idea of freedom of the press, its purpose, and its origins

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the benefits and challenges of media in a democracy.

description: Analyze current global issues (e.g., climate change, disease, food insecurity), explain ways people as global citizens can be involved (e.g., volunteering, advocacy, educating others), and argue the potential impacts

description: Explain how state government is funded

description: Argue the current and historic impact of the Rhode Island state government.

description: Explain ways people can create change in their local government (e.g., vote, petition, voice opinions at a hearing)

description: Analyze important local issues (e.g., public schools, public services, housing, land use, business regulation) and governmental response, and argue how local responses have impacted these issues

description: Explain the structure of the judiciary (e.g., eligibility, length of service, selection, and confirmation process)
